sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 47, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 46 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! LEFT changed the gray-bordered rect (node 12):
- Before: 85 pixels, weird boundary
- After LEFT: boundary changed from [[23, 24], [27, 28], [28, 28], [35, 21], [35, 20], [31, 16]] 
Wait, that boundary looks very strange. Let me look more carefully.
Before LEFT: id=12 c=W p=85 b=[[23, 24], [27, 28], [28, 28], [35, 21], [35, 20], [31, 16]]
After LEFT (which is RIGHT action): id=12 c=W p=84 b=[[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]]
Wait, the LEFT action changed the boundary and pixel count. The boundary before LEFT was weird - it had 6 points instead of 4, suggesting the shape changed. And pixels went from 84 to 85.
Then RIGHT changed it back: pixels 85→84, boundary back to normal.
So LEFT/RIGHT move or reshape the gray-bordered rect! This is a key discovery.
Let me explore this more. The gray-bordered rect seems to be movable with arrow keys. Let me check:
- LEFT: moved it left? The boundary changed significantly.
- RIGHT: moved it back.
- UP: no impact (maybe it was already at the top of its range)
- DOWN: I tried earlier and it only changed the HUD
Wait, but the boundary before LEFT was: [[23, 24], [27, 28], [28, 28], [35, 21], [35, 20], [31, 16]]
This is a very irregular shape with 6 corner points. That's weird for a rectangle. Unless the rect got distorted.
Hmm wait, I need to be more careful. The state before LEFT was after the UP action. Let me check if UP actually did nothing or if there was a prior issue.
Actually, looking at the output more carefully:
- "Before arrow keys" shows id=12 c=W p=84 (normal state)
- After UP: id=12 c=W p=84 (same, UP had no impact)
- After LEFT: id=12 c=W p=85 with weird boundary
- After RIGHT: id=12 c=W p=84 with normal boundary
So LEFT changed the rect's shape/size (84→85 pixels) and RIGHT changed it back. This suggests LEFT/RIGHT resize the rect horizontally, not move it.
Wait, 84 = 7×12 and 85 = ? Let me check: 85 = 5×17 or 17×5. That doesn't make sense for a resize.
Actually, 84 = 7×12. If LEFT adds 1 pixel, it becomes 85. If RIGHT subtracts 1 pixel, it goes back to 84.
So LEFT/RIGHT might resize the rect by 1 pixel at a time!
Let me test this hypothesis more carefully. But I'm running low on actions (46 of ~55).
Actually wait - I need to be really careful. The boundary after LEFT was: [[23, 24], [27, 28], [28, 28], [35, 21], [35, 20], [31, 16]]. This has 6 points which is unusual for a rectangle. Let me look at the ascii to understand the shape.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me look at the ascii around the gray-bordered rect to understand its shape
# Current state: after RIGHT, should be back to normal
# Let me try LEFT again and check the ascii

# First check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] in [8, 11, 12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"id={n['id']} c={n['color']} p={n['pixels']} b={n['boundary']}")

# Try LEFT and examine the ascii
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"\nAfter LEFT:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] in [8, 11, 12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"id={n['id']} c={n['color']} p={n['pixels']} b={n['boundary']}")

# Look at the ascii in the rect area
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if 20 <= i <= 35 and any(c != ' ' and c != 'B' for c in line[15:45]):
        print(f"Row {i}: {line[15:45]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
